# **The Synergistic Information Primitive Network (SIP-Net): A Biologically Plausible Architecture for Interpretable and Efficient Artificial Cognition**

## **The Algorithmic Gap in Modern Artificial Intelligence**

The pursuit of artificial general intelligence has historically relied on scaling homogeneous neural architectures, most notably Transformers and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). While these models achieve remarkable performance across diverse domains, they remain fundamentally opaque and computationally exhaustive. Current architectures operate as monolithic "black boxes," optimizing global loss functions via backpropagation across dense, uniform layers. This paradigm fails to differentiate the distinct algorithmic operations required for complex cognition, forcing all nodes to simultaneously attempt the storage, routing, and modification of data.1 In contrast, biological systems do not rely on uniform global optimization; cognitive functions such as selective attention, working memory, and stimulus representation emerge from the coordinated execution of low-level, specialized "information processing primitives" (IPPs).3

According to David Marr’s seminal framework, neural systems operate across three conceptual levels: the functional level (the cognitive task), the structural level (the anatomical wiring), and the algorithmic level (the intermediate information processing operations).3 Modern artificial neural networks (ANNs) effectively bridge the structural and functional levels but largely obscure the algorithmic level. By introducing a new theoretical paradigm based on Information Theory and Partial Information Decomposition (PID), it is possible to deconstruct high-level functions into elemental IPPs.3

The Synergistic Information Primitive Network (SIP-Net) formalizes this biological reality into an artificial architecture. Moving away from generic hidden layers, SIP-Net is constructed as a heterogeneous graph of specialized computational modules dedicated exclusively to specific information primitives. These modules are explicitly regularized during training to maximize distinct information-theoretic metrics—Active Information Storage (AIS), Transfer Entropy (TE), and Synergistic Information Modification—ensuring that the network's structural components align directly with their algorithmic roles.3 This report details the theoretical foundations, architectural design, mathematical training mechanisms, and step-by-step operational execution of the SIP-Net, utilizing a complex Natural Language Processing (NLP) task as a primary demonstration.

## **Theoretical Foundations: Information Theory and Neural Dynamics**

The foundational premise of the SIP-Net is that neural computations can be rigorously quantified and structurally segregated using information-theoretic functionals. Information theory, initially developed to optimize communication through noisy channels, provides a rigorous mathematical framework to study how multiple sources of information contribute to the correct prediction of a target variable—a multivariate information problem.1

To detect the presence and quantify the degree to which different network nodes engage in specific cognitive tasks, SIP-Net relies on elaborations of Shannon Entropy. The average information carried by an observation of a random variable ![][image1] is quantified by its entropy, ![][image2], while the mutual information (MI) between variables ![][image1] and ![][image3], ![][image4], quantifies their statistical dependence.3 However, standard mutual information is symmetric and bivariate, rendering it insufficient to capture the directional flow and multi-source integration characteristics of complex cognition. To address this, SIP-Net utilizes advanced information dynamics metrics to isolate the three core IPPs: buffering (storage), transferring (routing), and integrating (modification).3

| Information Processing Primitive | Cognitive Correlate | Information-Theoretic Metric | Mathematical Definition |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Buffering (Storage)** | Working Memory / Maintenance | Active Information Storage (AIS) | ![][image5] |
| **Transferring (Routing)** | Stimulus Propagation / Attention | Transfer Entropy (TE) | ![][image6] |
| **Integrating (Modification)** | Reasoning / Top-Down Modulation | Synergistic Information (PID) | ![][image7] |

### **Active Information Storage: The Mathematics of Memory**

In biological circuits, a node buffering information continues carrying information that was already present in its past states. This primitive processing operation is quantified by Active Information Storage (AIS), which measures the fraction of information carried by a node's activity at time ![][image8] that was already present at an earlier time ![][image9].3

In its simplest manifestation, AIS corresponds to the local mutual information between the past and present activity of a neural signal. Furthermore, one can evaluate the fraction of information regarding a specific stimulus (e.g., a specific input feature or context) actively buffered by a node. This results in stimulus-specific active storage, calculated as the totally stored information minus the part of this stored information that does not depend on the specific stimulus orientation.3 This corresponds to the co-information (or negative interaction information) among the variables. High AIS values indicate that a neural population is effectively isolating and maintaining data over time, independent of ongoing external fluctuations, which is the mathematical hallmark of working memory.3

### **Transfer Entropy: Quantifying Directed Information Flow**

The transfer of information between neural populations is assessed using Transfer Entropy (TE), a model-free measure of directed information transfer based on the Wiener-Granger causality principle.3 TE identifies information transfer between time series when future values of a target signal ![][image3] can be predicted from the past values of a source signal ![][image1], above and beyond what can be achieved from the autocorrelation of the target signal's own past.3

TE is fundamentally asymmetric (![][image10]), making it highly suitable for establishing directed functional connectivity across different structural domains.3 By computing the conditional mutual information between the past of ![][image1] and the present of ![][image3], conditioned on the past of ![][image3], TE isolates the actual transmission of novel, predictive information.16 In large-scale models of the cerebral cortex, phenomena such as feed-forward propagation of sensory stimuli are characterized by sharp spikes in TE from lower-order to higher-order regions.3

### **Partial Information Decomposition: Isolating Synergy**

The most complex primitive operation occurs when multiple streams of information converge on a single target, a process governed by Partial Information Decomposition (PID).1 When two source variables, ![][image11] and ![][image12], communicate with a target ![][image3], PID dissects the total mutual information into four distinct non-negative atoms:

1. **Unique Information (![][image13])**: Information provided solely by ![][image11].  
2. **Unique Information (![][image14])**: Information provided solely by ![][image12].  
3. **Redundant Information (![][image15])**: Overlapping information provided by both sources.  
4. **Synergistic Information (![][image16])**: Surplus information that emerges only when ![][image11] and ![][image12] are integrated simultaneously.1

Synergistic modification represents true computation. It is the extraction of surplus information that was inaccessible when analyzing either source in isolation.3 The synergistic atom serves as a direct proxy for non-trivial computation, distinguishing simple signal aggregation from the generation of higher-order cognitive insights.20

## **The SIP-Net Architecture: Structural Components**

Modern deep neural networks suffer from the von Neumann bottleneck and excessive energy consumption due to their reliance on dense matrix multiplications across homogeneous layers.23 By translating the biological principles of IPPs into an artificial neural network, the SIP-Net architecture eliminates this homogeneity. A SIP-Net is a heterogeneous graph composed of three distinct computational units, each structurally optimized to execute a single information-theoretic primitive.

### **Storage Nodes (Memory Primitives)**

In conventional architectures such as Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks or Transformers, memory is managed through computationally expensive recursive gating mechanisms or exhaustive attention caches that scale quadratically with sequence length.24 In biological systems, however, short-term memory is often maintained via persistent neuron firing within localized networks exhibiting "Stationary Bump" (SB) dynamical regimes, where a localized bump of activity is self-sustained by local recurrent excitation long after the external stimulus has ceased.3

SIP-Net Storage Nodes replicate this biological mechanism. These modules are designed as highly recurrent, low-decay units explicitly optimized to maximize Active Information Storage (AIS).

* **Design**: Storage Nodes feature internal sparse recurrent matrices parameterized to operate near the edge of chaos, a state that maximizes memory capacity and local predictability.27 They utilize localized, threshold-linear transfer functions that allow for the maintenance of an activation state without continuous input.3  
* **Function**: Storage Nodes act as localized working memory. They ingest high-value contextual data and isolate it over time. Because they are structurally disconnected from the main feedforward pathways of the network, they hold context without passing it through deep, computationally expensive layers. The information remains quarantined in a high-AIS state until a computational center queries the node, mirroring the brain's ability to hold semantic context passively while processing active sensory input.3

### **Transfer Buses (Routing Primitives)**

In traditional multi-layer perceptrons or graph neural networks (GNNs), information is aggregated indiscriminately from all neighbors, frequently leading to the dilution of critical signals (oversmoothing) and the unnecessary transmission of redundant noise.30 Biological inter-areal communication, conversely, utilizes spatiotopically organized, high-fidelity projections that maintain signal integrity across hierarchical distances.3

SIP-Net introduces Transfer Buses to facilitate this directed communication.

* **Design**: Transfer Buses are high-fidelity, linear, or minimally non-linear connections linking sensory inputs, Storage Nodes, and computational hubs. They are algorithmically governed and structurally optimized for Transfer Entropy (TE).3  
* **Function**: The sole purpose of a Transfer Bus is routing. Utilizing mechanisms analogous to attention but restricted by causal information flow constraints, Transfer Buses move data from source to target without distorting the underlying manifold. By enforcing high TE, the network ensures that a Transfer Bus only activates if the source node provides statistically significant predictive information about the target node's future state, effectively acting as an information-theoretic pruning mechanism that eliminates redundant structural blurring.31

### **Synergy Hubs (Modification Primitives)**

Current artificial neural networks distribute computation amorphously. When a Transformer resolves a complex linguistic dependency, the specific layer or head responsible for the synthesis is often obscured by redundant operations occurring simultaneously across the network.2 In the mammalian neocortex, however, specific cortical intersections—such as the prefrontal integration of bottom-up sensory streams and top-down working memory—serve as dedicated computational loci.3

The SIP-Net localizes computation into Synergy Hubs.

* **Design**: Synergy Hubs are highly non-linear, sparse integrators. Drawing inspiration from "infomorphic" neuron models, these hubs possess distinct input compartments to independently aggregate feedforward data (via Transfer Buses) and contextual data (via Storage Nodes).4  
* **Function**: Synergy Hubs are the sole sites of actual computation. Governed by the principles of Partial Information Decomposition (PID), these hubs are explicitly designed to maximize Synergistic Information. They remain dormant when processing redundant or highly predictable data streams. They only fire when multiple distinct pieces of information converge—for example, synthesizing a current sensory input with past context buffered in a Storage Node. By extracting the surplus information that exists only in the joint distribution of these inputs, the Synergy Hub produces a novel output embedding, executing higher-order cognitive logic.3

## **The Training Mechanism: Information-Theoretic Regularization**

To compel the SIP-Net to utilize its structurally segregated IPPs, the training paradigm must move beyond simple error minimization. Standard optimization via backpropagation against a global loss function (e.g., Cross-Entropy) encourages the network to find the path of least resistance, typically resulting in highly entangled, redundant representations where single neurons process overlapping features indiscriminately.1

Biological systems build on self-organized, local learning, achieving robustness and efficiency with limited global information.2 To emulate this, the SIP-Net utilizes an Information Dynamics Regularizer integrated directly into the loss function, forcing the network to organize its internal state biologically.

### **The Composite Loss Function Formulation**

The training objective of the SIP-Net is defined by the following composite loss equation:

![][image17]  
In this multi-objective optimization framework:

* ![][image18] represents the standard prediction error (e.g., categorical cross-entropy for next-token prediction, or mean squared error for continuous targets).  
* The negative terms act as intrinsic reward signals, pushing the respective structural modules to maximize their assigned information-theoretic properties.  
* ![][image19] and ![][image20] are dynamic hyperparameter coefficients that regulate the importance of the IPP rewards throughout the training lifecycle.

To optimize this function via backpropagation, the information metrics must be differentiable. Historically, estimating quantities like TE and PID atoms required discrete binning or k-nearest neighbor (kNN) estimators, which suffered from the curse of dimensionality and non-differentiability.16 SIP-Net overcomes this by leveraging recent theoretical advances, such as the shared-exclusion PID (![][image21]) measure and continuous Gaussian approximations of joint latent spaces, which permit analytical calculation and direct backpropagation of gradients with respect to the node weights.4

### **Phases of Training and Representational Complexity**

The application of this information-theoretic regularization results in distinct, observable phases of learning, mirroring the cognitive development of complex systems. The concept of "Representational Complexity," which quantifies the difficulty of accessing information spread across multiple neurons, is utilized to track this evolution.43

1. **Phase 1: Redundant Encoding (High ![][image18], Low ![][image22])**: In the initial epochs, the ![][image22] coefficients are kept low. The network focuses entirely on minimizing ![][image18], rapidly developing basic feature detectors. During this phase, PID analysis reveals that the network stores vast amounts of redundant information, as multiple units independently learn to extract the same salient features, leading to high representational complexity.1  
2. **Phase 2: Pruning and Routing Optimization (Increasing ![][image23])**: As ![][image23] scales up, the Transfer Buses are penalized for transmitting redundant or non-predictive information. The network is forced to rely on high-fidelity, high-TE pathways, effectively sparsifying the connectivity graph and eliminating architectural dead weight.13  
3. **Phase 3: Specialization and Synergistic Emergence (Increasing ![][image24])**: In the final phase, the demand for high AIS forces the Storage Nodes to maintain critical context internally, offloading the burden of recurrent computation from the feedforward pathways.7 Simultaneously, the heavy weighting of ![][image20] forces the Synergy Hubs to specialize. Instead of relying on redundant signals, the hubs learn to extract unique and synergistic information. The representational complexity drops significantly as individual neurons assume distinct, non-overlapping roles.1

| Training Phase | Primary Objective | Dominant Information Profile | Structural Effect on SIP-Net |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **1\. Initialization** | Minimize ![][image18] | High Redundancy, Low Synergy | Amorphous representation; dense activation across all modules. |
| **2\. Routing Optimization** | Maximize ![][image25] | Increased Unique Information | Sparsification of Transfer Buses; elimination of redundant signal paths. |
| **3\. Functional Specialization** | Maximize ![][image26] & ![][image27] | High Synergy, High AIS | Segregation of memory to Storage Nodes; localization of logic to Synergy Hubs. |

## **Operationalizing SIP-Net: Step-by-Step Processing of a Complex NLP Task**

To practically illustrate the dynamic interplay of these specialized modules, it is necessary to trace the execution of a specific task through the SIP-Net architecture. Consider a complex Natural Language Processing (NLP) scenario involving long-distance coreference resolution and context-dependent semantic logic.

**The Input Sequence:**

*"The historic Washoe Smelter stack located in Anaconda, Montana, which was completed in 1918 to replace smaller structures and mitigate environmental damage, remains the tallest surviving masonry structure in the world, and today it..."*

The network is tasked with predicting the next logical sequence of tokens (e.g., "...stands as a monument," or "...serves as a landmark"). To succeed, the model must maintain the primary entity ("stack"), contextualize its location ("Anaconda, Montana"), process a massive subordinating clause ("completed in 1918... environmental damage"), and successfully associate the pronoun "it" with the original entity over a vast temporal lag.

In a standard dense Transformer architecture, this requires computing full ![][image28] attention matrices across the entire context window at every layer, repeatedly mixing the representations for "Anaconda," "1918," and "tallest" in a computationally exhaustive manner.25 The SIP-Net processes this input stream efficiently and interpretably through the precise orchestration of its IPPs.

### **Step 1: Sensory Encoding and Redundancy Filtering (Tokens: "The historic Washoe Smelter stack located in Anaconda, Montana")**

As the initial sequence of tokens is ingested, they are mapped to high-dimensional embeddings.

* **Transfer Bus Activation**: The embeddings are placed on the primary feedforward Transfer Buses. Because these buses are regularized by Transfer Entropy (TE), they evaluate the predictive utility of each token.11 Stop words ("The", "in") yield low TE and are aggressively down-weighted, preventing them from consuming downstream computational bandwidth.  
* **Entity Identification**: High-information tokens ("Washoe", "Smelter", "stack", "Anaconda", "Montana") generate strong TE signals. The network recognizes these as foundational contextual anchors.

### **Step 2: Buffering Core Context (Tokens: "which was completed in 1918 to replace smaller structures and mitigate environmental damage")**

As the network processes the lengthy subordinate clause, the fundamental challenge of working memory arises. The primary subject ("stack") must be remembered while unrelated semantic noise ("1918", "environmental damage") is processed.

* **Routing to Storage Nodes**: The Transfer Buses dynamically route the embeddings for "stack" and "Anaconda, Montana" away from the active processing stream and into dedicated Storage Nodes.  
* **Active Information Storage (AIS) Maintenance**: These Storage Nodes enter a highly recurrent state, optimizing their internal parameters to maximize AIS.3 The information is actively buffered. By sequestering this data, the SIP-Net prevents the "blurring" of the primary entity during the processing of the subordinate clause. The network avoids continuously calculating attention scores for "Anaconda" against tokens like "smaller" or "damage," saving immense computational energy.7

### **Step 3: Triggering Integration via Dynamic Assembly (Tokens: "remains the tallest surviving masonry structure in the world, and today it")**

The network ingests the final phase of the sequence, culminating in the pronoun "it".

* **Query Generation**: The token "it" carries low intrinsic semantic meaning but requires high context to resolve. This acts as a functional trigger.  
* **Top-Down Transfer Bus Activation**: A top-down Transfer Bus, bridging the Storage Nodes to the central computational sector, is activated. The buffered representations of "stack" and "Anaconda, Montana" are transmitted with high fidelity (high TE) to a specific Synergy Hub.3  
* **Bottom-Up Transfer Bus Activation**: Simultaneously, the current linguistic state ("tallest surviving masonry structure... today it") is passed via a bottom-up Transfer Bus to the same Synergy Hub.3

### **Step 4: Synergistic Computation and Output Generation**

The coreference resolution and semantic prediction occur strictly within the Synergy Hub.

* **Information Modification (Synergy)**: The Synergy Hub ingests the disparate streams. According to its PID-parameterized loss function, it must generate an output that possesses high Synergistic Information.4 A simple summation of the inputs would yield redundant information; instead, the Hub performs non-linear dendritic integration. It combines the physical entity ("masonry structure", "stack"), the location ("Anaconda, Montana"), and the temporal context ("today") into a novel, cohesive conceptual state.20  
* **Prediction**: This synergistically modified embedding heavily biases the final decoding layer, prioritizing contextually accurate tokens like "stands" or "serves" over grammatically possible but contextually inaccurate tokens (like "falls" or "melts").  
* **Loss Backpropagation**: The prediction is evaluated against the target. The network backpropagates the task loss (![][image18]), but simultaneously updates the Storage Nodes for their AIS retention capabilities, the Transfer Buses for their routing efficiency, and the Synergy Hub for its synergistic maximization, ensuring the structural modules remain highly specialized.2

| Sequential Phase | NLP Tokens Processed | Active SIP-Net Module | Dominant Information Primitive | Algorithmic Role |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **1\. Encoding** | "The historic Washoe Smelter stack... Anaconda, Montana" | Transfer Buses | Transfer Entropy (TE) | High-fidelity extraction and routing of core semantic entities. |
| **2\. Buffering** | "...which was completed in 1918 to replace smaller structures..." | Storage Nodes | Active Information Storage (AIS) | Passive maintenance of primary subjects; isolates context from ongoing syntactic noise. |
| **3\. Routing Query** | "...and today it" | Transfer Buses (Top-Down & Bottom-Up) | Transfer Entropy (TE) | Dynamic assembly of connections; routing historical memory and current state to integrators. |
| **4\. Integration** | \[Prediction Phase\] | Synergy Hubs | Synergistic Modification (PID) | Nonlinear integration of memory and sensory input to resolve coreference and predict next token. |

## **Transformative Advantages of the SIP-Net Architecture**

The transition from homogeneous, densely connected deep learning models to the heterogeneous, information-theoretically constrained SIP-Net offers profound, quantifiable advancements across three critical domains of artificial intelligence: interpretability, computational efficiency, and dynamic flexibility.

### **Extreme Interpretability and Structural Transparency**

Modern Large Language Models (LLMs) suffer from an intractable "black box" problem. When an LLM generates a hallucination or an incorrect output, diagnosing the exact point of failure is exceedingly difficult because representations are hopelessly entangled across billions of parameters. Feature attribution methods and attention-weight visualizations provide only superficial, post-hoc explanations.37

SIP-Net fundamentally resolves this via "extreme interpretability" rooted in its intrinsic architecture. Because the network forces information to be explicitly stored, transferred, or modified based on PID constraints, the internal state of the network can be queried mathematically, providing a transparent window into task-relevant local information processing.2 If the SIP-Net fails the NLP coreference resolution task described above, practitioners can interrogate the IPPs directly to isolate the failure mechanism:

* **Memory Failure**: Was the subject forgotten? The practitioner checks the Active Information Storage (AIS) metric of the designated Storage Nodes. A drop in AIS indicates a failure in the buffering primitive.3  
* **Routing Failure**: Was the information lost in transit? The practitioner measures the Transfer Entropy (TE) across the top-down Transfer Buses. A TE value approaching zero indicates a break in communication.12  
* **Logic Failure**: Did the network fail to synthesize the data? The practitioner examines the PID atoms of the Synergy Hubs. If the calculation reveals high Redundant Information but low Synergistic Information, the hub failed to execute the complex modification required for logic.2

By framing the model's computations as measurable information atoms, SIP-Net transforms interpretability from an external guessing game into an intrinsic, mathematically verifiable property of the network.37

### **Energy Efficiency and Biological Sparsity**

The computational demands and energy consumption of current AI architectures scale exponentially, primarily driven by dense matrix multiplications and the massive memory access overhead required by self-attention mechanisms.36 In contrast, the biological human brain consumes approximately 20 watts. This remarkable efficiency is achieved through extreme sparsity; neurons fire only when required, and computation is highly localized.47

SIP-Net replicates biological energy efficiency by enforcing structural sparsity through its IPPs. In a standard Transformer, every token continuously interacts with every other token through deep multi-head attention layers, regardless of the token's semantic value.25 In SIP-Net, computation is restricted and aggressively localized:

1. **Passive Maintenance over Active Computation**: Storage Nodes maintain context via low-energy recurrent loops that maximize AIS, entirely circumventing the need to push historical embeddings through continuous, deep matrix multiplications.24  
2. **Information-Theoretic Pruning**: Transfer Buses are regularized by TE. If the Transfer Entropy from node ![][image1] to node ![][image3] is zero—meaning ![][image1] provides no predictive information about ![][image3]—the connection is functionally pruned. This mimics the energy-efficient event-driven routing seen in advanced Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs), significantly reducing data movement overhead.46  
3. **Concentrated Logic**: Expensive, highly non-linear computations are isolated exclusively to Synergy Hubs. Because the network is trained to maximize synergy, these hubs remain dormant during routine data transfer and activate only when complex multi-source integration is strictly required.4

This rigorous compartmentalization heavily reduces the aggregate floating-point operations (FLOPs) required during inference, making the SIP-Net an ideal, low-power candidate for deployment in energy-constrained environments or on neuromorphic hardware.29

### **Dynamic Assembly and Architectural Flexibility**

Biological cognition is characterized by fluidity; the brain can rapidly transition from a state of highly focused sensory processing to abstract, internalized reasoning. This flexibility is supported by the "dynome"—the collection of dynamic modes that a neural circuit can support using the same underlying structural connectome.3

Standard ANNs generally require distinct architectures or catastrophic parameter shifts (fine-tuning) to accommodate different task modalities. SIP-Net, however, leverages the concept of Dynamic Assembly. Because high-level cognitive functions are simply different temporal combinations of foundational IPPs, the SIP-Net can dynamically route connections to alter its macroscopic behavior without altering its learned weights.3

For example, the network can instantly shift its functional state based on task demands:

* **In an "Attention/Reflex" Task**: The network requires high sensory throughput and rapid responses. SIP-Net dynamically increases the weighting of Transfer Buses (maximizing TE) while bypassing Storage Nodes, creating a highly reactive, feedforward configuration similar to the "Strong Global Balanced Amplification" seen in primate visual cortices.3  
* **In a "Reasoning/Logic" Task**: The network requires deep synthesis of historical data. SIP-Net engages the Storage Nodes (maximizing AIS) and aggressively routes delayed data to Synergy Hubs, pausing output generation until synergistic integration peaks.38

This macro-level flexibility allows a single SIP-Net architecture to act as a generalized reasoning engine, capable of adapting its internal information dynamics to meet the specific algorithmic requirements of highly divergent cognitive tasks.

## **Synthesis and Future Outlook**

The Synergistic Information Primitive Network (SIP-Net) represents a fundamental paradigm shift in the design of artificial neural systems. By moving decisively away from the homogeneous, black-box architectures that define current AI and embracing the biological reality of segregated information processing, SIP-Net establishes a rigorous pathway toward more robust, understandable, and computationally elegant artificial cognition.

Through the explicit application of Information Theory—specifically Active Information Storage, Transfer Entropy, and Partial Information Decomposition—SIP-Net ensures that every structural component of the network serves a mathematically definable algorithmic purpose. The architecture guarantees that Storage Nodes actively buffer context, Transfer Buses route predictive signals without distortion, and Synergy Hubs compute novel, higher-order representations.3

As demonstrated through the granular execution of a complex NLP task, this IPP-driven approach resolves the critical inefficiencies of modern deep learning. By enforcing biological sparsity, SIP-Net drastically reduces the energy footprint and FLOP requirements of inference. By facilitating dynamic assembly, it enables fluid, real-time task-switching without structural retraining. Most importantly, by parameterizing the composite loss function with differentiable information metrics, SIP-Net shatters the opaque nature of deep learning, providing researchers and practitioners with an intrinsically interpretable, mathematically transparent window into the mechanics of artificial reasoning.2 As the theoretical models of "infomorphic" learning and IPP-based neural circuits continue to mature, the SIP-Net stands as a highly viable, scientifically grounded blueprint for the next generation of advanced, generalizable computational systems.
